# Human Positioning Relative to AI Coding Agents

The central question in AI-assisted software development is where humans should position themselves relative to the work that coding agents perform. Three distinct postures have been identified — **humans outside the loop**, **humans in the loop**, and **humans on the loop** — each with different tradeoffs for productivity, quality, and scalability. The recommended posture — on the loop — gives rise to a specific discipline, **harness engineering**, and ultimately to a self-reinforcing **agentic flywheel** that compounds improvements over time.

### Humans outside the loop

This posture corresponds to vibe coding or certain interpretations of Spec Driven Development (SDD), where humans focus entirely on specifying desired outcomes and leave agents to handle all implementation details. The appeal is that the outcome-focused "why loop" — iterating over ideas and working software — is what humans actually care about. Software development is messy, prone to over-engineering and technical debt, and each new LLM generation improves at turning prompts into working software. If the output is unsatisfactory, the human simply tells the LLM and gets another iteration. The risk is that without any human inspection of intermediate artifacts, structural problems compound silently until they become expensive to fix.

### Humans in the loop

In this posture, developers act as gatekeepers within the innermost code-generation loop, manually inspecting each line of LLM-produced code. Human experience and judgment still exceed LLMs in many situations — a developer can often fix a spiraling agent's problem in seconds. However, this approach creates bottlenecks. Reports on developer productivity with AI show mixed results, potentially because humans spend more time specifying and reviewing code than they save from AI generation. As agent output volume increases, in-the-loop review becomes unsustainable without either lowering standards or limiting throughput.

### Humans on the loop

This is presented as the recommended middle path. Rather than personally inspecting agent output at the code level, humans build and maintain the **harness** — the collection of specifications, quality checks, and workflow guidance that govern the different levels of loops inside the how loop. The key distinction from "in the loop" is what happens when output is unsatisfactory: instead of fixing the artifact directly, the human changes the harness that produced it, so future outputs improve systematically. This is a systemic rather than ad hoc approach to quality. The posture scales because improving the harness is a one-time investment that pays off across all subsequent agent runs.

The discipline that has formed around this posture is **harness engineering** — the practice of building and continuously refining the systems that control how AI agents operate within the software development process. The harness engineer's instinct, when confronted with an unsatisfactory artifact, is never to patch the artifact but to modify the harness so it produces better results going forward. This is the mechanism through which humans work "on the loop" rather than in it.

### The agentic flywheel

The on-the-loop posture reaches its most powerful expression in the **agentic flywheel**, where humans direct agents to manage and improve the harness itself rather than doing so manually. The flywheel is built by giving agents the information they need to evaluate loop performance. A starting point is the tests and evaluations already embedded in the harness, but the flywheel becomes more powerful with richer signals: pipeline stages measuring performance, failure scenario validation, operational production data, user journey logs, and commercial results.

At each workflow step, agents review results and recommend harness improvements — including improvements to upstream parts of the workflow. This creates a self-improving agent harness. Initially, humans consider recommendations interactively and prompt agents to implement specific changes. Recommendations can also be added to the product backlog for prioritization. Agents can assign scores to recommendations including risks, costs, and benefits, and recommendations meeting certain score thresholds can be automatically approved and applied.

Over time, particularly for standard, frequently-performed work where improvement loops reach diminishing returns, this may come to resemble vibe coding from the human's perspective. But the crucial difference is that the harness underneath has been engineered through accumulated feedback — producing not just one-off "good enough" solutions but robust, potentially anti-fragile systems that continuously improve themselves.

### The why loop and the how loop

The underlying framework distinguishes between a **"why loop"** (iterating over ideas and outcomes, always human-driven) and a **"how loop"** (the process of building software, which contains multiple nested loops from high-level specification down to code generation and testing). Intermediate artifacts — code, tests, documentation, technical designs, ADRs — are means to an end rather than deliverables in themselves. Practices, architectural approaches, and design patterns like microservices or CUPID are similarly instrumental. The three postures differ in how deeply the human engages with the how loop: outside-the-loop humans engage only with the why loop, in-the-loop humans engage with every nested layer of the how loop, and on-the-loop humans engage selectively with the how loop by shaping the harness rather than the artifacts it produces. The agentic flywheel extends this further — the human's engagement with the how loop becomes increasingly mediated by agents that themselves propose and implement harness changes, with the human retaining directional authority over the why loop and approval authority over significant harness modifications.

## See Also

- [[z20260315-001|Agent Harness Architecture]] — details the harness infrastructure that defines the "on the loop" posture, including tools, documentation, and feedback mechanisms that the harness engineer builds and the agentic flywheel refines
- [[z20260315-008|The Transformation of the Software Engineering Role in the Age of AI Coding Agents]] — describes how the engineering role is evolving in practice across the postures outlined here, with planning, quality enforcement, and orchestration as primary activities
- [[z20260315-003|Long-Horizon Autonomous Agent Execution]] — illustrates the autonomous execution patterns that make the "outside the loop" and "on the loop" postures feasible at scale
